title: #architecture: 1739566123
preview:
Ravi Menon: Kicking off review for ADR-027 (throughput SLO metric definition). Need sign-off from SRE/Obs/Infra/Product before we lock it + route ENG work. Link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-027-throughput-slo-metric-definition

Ravi Menon: TL;DR proposal: canonical metric = tokens/sec at the deployment boundary, attributed per `deployment_id` (Dedicated), evaluated over sliding window (not per-request). Requests/sec stays as secondary/supporting metric.

Mina Haddad: +1 to per-deployment. Routing needs an unambiguous unit to protect, otherwise tier-aware admission control becomes guessy. One thing: we should call out whether tokens include prompt+completion (total) or completion only.

Sean Gallagher: Read it. Main concerns:
1) streaming accounting (we already saw double-counting in early metrics),
2) batching/caching: tokens/sec can look “too good” if cache hits aren’t separated,
3) multi-model deployments (same deployment_id w/ 2 models) -> attribution gets messy.

Allison Grant: For instrumentation, we should be explicit on labels/aggregation: `deployment_id`, `model`, `model_version`, `region`, `route`.
If the ADR says “per deployment”, we still need `model` dimension to debug + capacity plan.

Rafael Mendes: For alerting, I want the ADR to say what we page on:
- SLO signal = min(throughput floor compliance over 5m) AND sustained over 30m for incident
- burn-rate windows rely on consistent denominator
If the metric definition is ambiguous, burn-rate templates will be noisy.

Irene Choi: Infra POV: tokens/sec is fine but ONLY if we constrain the measurement conditions. Shape variance across A100/H100/L40S is big, esp long context. We need ADR language that the SLO metric is evaluated against a pinned model+version+max_ctx and “supported config” list.

Quentin Harris: +1. Please add a sentence: if customer changes model, quantization profile, or context beyond supported limits, the throughput guarantee (and metric target) needs re-baselining.
Also: clarify whether “tokens” are post-tokenization model tokens (not characters).

Ethan Park: Control plane needs a clear “source of truth” for which config is “the deployment.” Today customers can change `max_batch_size` + `max_concurrency` without reprovisioning. Do we treat those as customer-tunable knobs that can invalidate the SLO, or do we lock them per tier?

Lauren Bishop: From product side, ADR shouldn’t read like contract language, but it should align with the PRD so we don’t end up promising “completion tokens/sec” in one place and “total tokens/sec” in another.
My vote: define canonical throughput as total tokens/sec (prompt+completion) at the API boundary, because customers care about end-to-end cost + time.

Sergio Costa: Dashboarding note: if we pick total tokens/sec, we need panels for prompt vs completion split anyway. Otherwise we’ll get escalations where a customer says “my output is slow” but their prompts got longer and total tokens/sec looks fine.

Sean Gallagher: Re: total vs completion-only: SRE preference is total tokens/sec for simplicity + fewer edge cases. But we must specify the counting point:
- count prompt tokens once per request at request accept
- count completion tokens as generated (streaming chunks)
- exclude retransmits/retries where client disconnects? (or at least define)

Mina Haddad: On disconnects: routing/admission control sees the request as accepted. If client drops mid-stream, we probably should count tokens actually produced, but not “expected” tokens. Otherwise you can get weird incentives.

Allison Grant: We can implement “generated_tokens_total” at server-side and “request_prompt_tokens” at accept. For disconnect, generated tokens stops. That’s clean.
Also please add: metric is computed from server-side counters, not client reported.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also add guidance that we de-dupe on `request_id` for retries where we can (server retry) and otherwise rely on idempotency keys.

Irene Choi: There’s also warmup/warm pool. First N minutes after deploy / scale-up is not representative. ADR should mention whether we exclude warmup time from SLO computations or whether the SLO target assumes warm pool exists for Plus/Premium.

Lauren Bishop: Warm pool policy is tiered (Premium yes, Standard not guaranteed). Let’s keep warmup exclusions in the SLO definition doc, but ADR can note that “measurement assumes deployment is in steady state and serving,” and point to where warmup is handled.

Sofia Mendes: Legal hat: agree ADR shouldn’t be contract text, but please avoid wording like “guarantee” in ADR. Use “SLO target” / “measurement signal.” If you add exclusions, be careful to phrase as “measurement scope” not “we don’t care.”

Kira Thompson: I can help edit for clarity. If we’re aligning docs, can we add a short glossary section:
- token (model token)
- prompt token vs completion token
- throughput signal vs SLO target
- deployment boundary

Quentin Harris: Also call out that tokenizer depends on model family. Metric uses the model’s tokenizer (or Redwood standard for that model). Otherwise “tokens/sec” is meaningless across models.

Ethan Park: For the “deployment boundary”: is it at the public API ingress or at the model worker? If at worker, routing retries/fallback can skew. If at ingress, we include queueing + admission effects.

Sean Gallagher: For SLO measurement, I think we want at the service boundary where customer sees performance (ingress). But the *throughput* is token generation, so it’s mostly worker-side. Proposed compromise:
- count tokens at worker generation (source of truth)
- attribute to deployment_id at ingress decision
That way fallback still counts against the deployment that accepted it (unless we explicitly define fallback semantics).

Mina Haddad: If we do that, ADR must specify fallback attribution. For Dedicated, fallback to a different pool/region should still count against the same deployment_id (customer’s Dedicated deployment) for SLO reporting, otherwise we “fix” misses by shifting traffic.

Rafael Mendes: +1, otherwise SLO dashboards lie and incident review is painful.

Sergio Costa: Noted. For dashboards, we can show both:
- “logical deployment throughput” (counts fallback)
- “physical pool throughput” (where it actually ran)
Helps debugging.

Ravi Menon: This is great. Action items I’m hearing for ADR text:
1) Define token counting (prompt once at accept, completion as generated; total tokens/sec canonical)
2) Clarify attribution: logical `deployment_id` at ingress decision; fallback counts toward same deployment_id; add optional “physical execution” dimension
3) Call out required labels: deployment_id/model/model_version/region/route
4) Note steady-state assumption + link out to warmup handling (tier-dependent)
5) Add glossary + avoid “guarantee” language

Ravi Menon: I’ll push edits today + tag for final sign-off. Target: EOD Thu.

ADR Bot: Ravi Menon updated “ADR-027-throughput-slo-metric-definition” (v7) in Confluence. Review requested from @Sean Gallagher, @Allison Grant, @Irene Choi, @Lauren Bishop, @Sofia Mendes.

Sean Gallagher: Reviewed v7. Looks good. Only nit: add one line that cached tokens are still “tokens” but we’ll also emit cache hit rate separately; SLO metric doesn’t try to back out caching.

Allison Grant: v7 LGTM. Added a comment on label cardinality (route vs endpoint) to avoid blowing up metrics. We can use `deployment_route_class` instead of raw path.

Irene Choi: v7 LGTM from Infra. Thanks for adding the “supported config/model pinning” note.

Lauren Bishop: v7 good. Please just ensure PRD + docs PR use the same “total tokens/sec” phrasing.

Sofia Mendes: v7 ok from Legal. Appreciate the wording changes.

Ravi Menon: Awesome. I’ll mark ADR as Accepted once I incorporate Sean’s caching sentence + Allison’s route_class suggestion. Thanks all :thumbsup:
text:
Ravi Menon: Kicking off review for ADR-027 (throughput SLO metric definition). Need sign-off from SRE/Obs/Infra/Product before we lock it + route ENG work. Link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-027-throughput-slo-metric-definition

Ravi Menon: TL;DR proposal: canonical metric = tokens/sec at the deployment boundary, attributed per `deployment_id` (Dedicated), evaluated over sliding window (not per-request). Requests/sec stays as secondary/supporting metric.

Mina Haddad: +1 to per-deployment. Routing needs an unambiguous unit to protect, otherwise tier-aware admission control becomes guessy. One thing: we should call out whether tokens include prompt+completion (total) or completion only.

Sean Gallagher: Read it. Main concerns:
1) streaming accounting (we already saw double-counting in early metrics),
2) batching/caching: tokens/sec can look “too good” if cache hits aren’t separated,
3) multi-model deployments (same deployment_id w/ 2 models) -> attribution gets messy.

Allison Grant: For instrumentation, we should be explicit on labels/aggregation: `deployment_id`, `model`, `model_version`, `region`, `route`.
If the ADR says “per deployment”, we still need `model` dimension to debug + capacity plan.

Rafael Mendes: For alerting, I want the ADR to say what we page on:
- SLO signal = min(throughput floor compliance over 5m) AND sustained over 30m for incident
- burn-rate windows rely on consistent denominator
If the metric definition is ambiguous, burn-rate templates will be noisy.

Irene Choi: Infra POV: tokens/sec is fine but ONLY if we constrain the measurement conditions. Shape variance across A100/H100/L40S is big, esp long context. We need ADR language that the SLO metric is evaluated against a pinned model+version+max_ctx and “supported config” list.

Quentin Harris: +1. Please add a sentence: if customer changes model, quantization profile, or context beyond supported limits, the throughput guarantee (and metric target) needs re-baselining.
Also: clarify whether “tokens” are post-tokenization model tokens (not characters).

Ethan Park: Control plane needs a clear “source of truth” for which config is “the deployment.” Today customers can change `max_batch_size` + `max_concurrency` without reprovisioning. Do we treat those as customer-tunable knobs that can invalidate the SLO, or do we lock them per tier?

Lauren Bishop: From product side, ADR shouldn’t read like contract language, but it should align with the PRD so we don’t end up promising “completion tokens/sec” in one place and “total tokens/sec” in another.
My vote: define canonical throughput as total tokens/sec (prompt+completion) at the API boundary, because customers care about end-to-end cost + time.

Sergio Costa: Dashboarding note: if we pick total tokens/sec, we need panels for prompt vs completion split anyway. Otherwise we’ll get escalations where a customer says “my output is slow” but their prompts got longer and total tokens/sec looks fine.

Sean Gallagher: Re: total vs completion-only: SRE preference is total tokens/sec for simplicity + fewer edge cases. But we must specify the counting point:
- count prompt tokens once per request at request accept
- count completion tokens as generated (streaming chunks)
- exclude retransmits/retries where client disconnects? (or at least define)

Mina Haddad: On disconnects: routing/admission control sees the request as accepted. If client drops mid-stream, we probably should count tokens actually produced, but not “expected” tokens. Otherwise you can get weird incentives.

Allison Grant: We can implement “generated_tokens_total” at server-side and “request_prompt_tokens” at accept. For disconnect, generated tokens stops. That’s clean.
Also please add: metric is computed from server-side counters, not client reported.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also add guidance that we de-dupe on `request_id` for retries where we can (server retry) and otherwise rely on idempotency keys.

Irene Choi: There’s also warmup/warm pool. First N minutes after deploy / scale-up is not representative. ADR should mention whether we exclude warmup time from SLO computations or whether the SLO target assumes warm pool exists for Plus/Premium.

Lauren Bishop: Warm pool policy is tiered (Premium yes, Standard not guaranteed). Let’s keep warmup exclusions in the SLO definition doc, but ADR can note that “measurement assumes deployment is in steady state and serving,” and point to where warmup is handled.

Sofia Mendes: Legal hat: agree ADR shouldn’t be contract text, but please avoid wording like “guarantee” in ADR. Use “SLO target” / “measurement signal.” If you add exclusions, be careful to phrase as “measurement scope” not “we don’t care.”

Kira Thompson: I can help edit for clarity. If we’re aligning docs, can we add a short glossary section:
- token (model token)
- prompt token vs completion token
- throughput signal vs SLO target
- deployment boundary

Quentin Harris: Also call out that tokenizer depends on model family. Metric uses the model’s tokenizer (or Redwood standard for that model). Otherwise “tokens/sec” is meaningless across models.

Ethan Park: For the “deployment boundary”: is it at the public API ingress or at the model worker? If at worker, routing retries/fallback can skew. If at ingress, we include queueing + admission effects.

Sean Gallagher: For SLO measurement, I think we want at the service boundary where customer sees performance (ingress). But the *throughput* is token generation, so it’s mostly worker-side. Proposed compromise:
- count tokens at worker generation (source of truth)
- attribute to deployment_id at ingress decision
That way fallback still counts against the deployment that accepted it (unless we explicitly define fallback semantics).

Mina Haddad: If we do that, ADR must specify fallback attribution. For Dedicated, fallback to a different pool/region should still count against the same deployment_id (customer’s Dedicated deployment) for SLO reporting, otherwise we “fix” misses by shifting traffic.

Rafael Mendes: +1, otherwise SLO dashboards lie and incident review is painful.

Sergio Costa: Noted. For dashboards, we can show both:
- “logical deployment throughput” (counts fallback)
- “physical pool throughput” (where it actually ran)
Helps debugging.

Ravi Menon: This is great. Action items I’m hearing for ADR text:
1) Define token counting (prompt once at accept, completion as generated; total tokens/sec canonical)
2) Clarify attribution: logical `deployment_id` at ingress decision; fallback counts toward same deployment_id; add optional “physical execution” dimension
3) Call out required labels: deployment_id/model/model_version/region/route
4) Note steady-state assumption + link out to warmup handling (tier-dependent)
5) Add glossary + avoid “guarantee” language

Ravi Menon: I’ll push edits today + tag for final sign-off. Target: EOD Thu.

ADR Bot: Ravi Menon updated “ADR-027-throughput-slo-metric-definition” (v7) in Confluence. Review requested from @Sean Gallagher, @Allison Grant, @Irene Choi, @Lauren Bishop, @Sofia Mendes.

Sean Gallagher: Reviewed v7. Looks good. Only nit: add one line that cached tokens are still “tokens” but we’ll also emit cache hit rate separately; SLO metric doesn’t try to back out caching.

Allison Grant: v7 LGTM. Added a comment on label cardinality (route vs endpoint) to avoid blowing up metrics. We can use `deployment_route_class` instead of raw path.

Irene Choi: v7 LGTM from Infra. Thanks for adding the “supported config/model pinning” note.

Lauren Bishop: v7 good. Please just ensure PRD + docs PR use the same “total tokens/sec” phrasing.

Sofia Mendes: v7 ok from Legal. Appreciate the wording changes.

Ravi Menon: Awesome. I’ll mark ADR as Accepted once I incorporate Sean’s caching sentence + Allison’s route_class suggestion. Thanks all :thumbsup:

title: #eng-oncall: 1739563228
preview:
Rafael Mendes: on-call handoff for audit exporter incident from earlier (lag spike / downstream 429s). capturing what to watch + toggles. cc @Aisha Bello (taking primary)

Redwood Incident Bot: Incident INC-4471 status changed: MITIGATED → MONITORING

Rafael Mendes: TL;DR
- root cause: Splunk HEC rate-limiting (429) on 2 beta tenants → retries/backoff built up queue → export lag peaked ~38m
- mitigation: paused Splunk adapter for affected tenants + reduced batch size + verified no DLQ growth
- current: lag back < 5m; retry rate normalizing

Rafael Mendes: Dashboards to keep open tonight:
1) Grafana: Audit Exporter / Overview (lag + throughput)
   https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/audit-exporter-overview
   Panels to watch:
   - "p95 export lag (by destination)"
   - "events exported/sec (by tenant)"
   - "checkpoint age"
2) Grafana: Audit Exporter / Destinations
   https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/audit-exporter-destinations
   Panels:
   - "adapter HTTP status codes" (look for sustained 429/403)
   - "retry attempts" + "backoff seconds"
3) Queue/Storage dashboard (exporter internal)
   https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/audit-exporter-queues
   Panels:
   - "pending batches" (should not trend up)
   - "DLQ depth" (should be ~0; anything non-zero means investigate)

Aisha Bello: got it. what are the paging thresholds we’re using right now?

Rafael Mendes: from SRE draft (we used these in the incident):
- page if p95 export lag > 15m for 10m (any destination) OR > 30m for 5m (critical)
- page if delivery success rate < 99.5% over 30m (per adapter)
- ticket-only if 429s elevated but lag < 10m
(Sean can confirm if we’re tweaking, but this is what alert rules are set to as of today)

Sean Gallagher: confirming. alert rules match. note: we loosened 429 warning to ticket-only after the noise last week.

Rafael Mendes: Toggles / controls (use sparingly; prefer tenant-scoped first):
A) Pause exports per destination type (safe):
- control-plane admin: `POST /internal/audit-exports/{tenant_id}/pause` with body `{"destination":"splunk"}`
- UI (Console → Audit Exports → Actions → Pause) works if you have the SRE role
This stops new sends but keeps checkpoints; lag metric will stop increasing but backlog remains.

B) Global kill switch (last resort):
- feature flag: `siem_audit_export_beta_enabled` (tenant allowlist). DO NOT disable globally unless we’re melting down.
- if you must, flip via internal admin tool: https://admin.redwood.internal/flags/siem_audit_export_beta_enabled

C) Backpressure safety / auto-pause:
- exporter has `maxLagAutoPauseMinutes=60` (from PR-1119). If lag hits this, it auto-pauses the destination and emits an error.
- when it triggers, you’ll see alert "destination auto-paused" + log line `AUTO_PAUSE_MAX_LAG`.

Aisha Bello: if we pause, what’s the expected customer impact message?

Rafael Mendes: "exports temporarily paused due to downstream rate limiting; no audit events deleted; once resumed exporter will backfill from last checkpoint; duplicates possible (at-least-once)". don’t promise exact timestamps.

Logan Wright: adding: if customer asks "missing events", point them to dedup via `event_id` on their side. at-least-once semantics.

Rafael Mendes: Quick triage checklist if lag starts climbing again:
1) Identify adapter causing it:
   - check "p95 export lag by destination" + "HTTP status codes"
2) If 429s from Splunk:
   - confirm which tenant(s): panel "429s by tenant"
   - reduce pressure first (tenant pause Splunk) rather than global
3) If 403s from Datadog:
   - usually API key scope / wrong site (datadoghq.com vs datadoghq.eu). see SUP-18497 notes.
4) If S3 errors:
   - look for `AccessDenied` or KMS issues; often rotated role trust or KMS key policy.

Rafael Mendes: Commands / where to look:
- exporter logs (prod):
  `kubectl -n audit-exporter logs deploy/audit-exporter -c exporter --since=30m | rg "ERROR|429|AUTO_PAUSE|DLQ"`
- check tenant config state:
  `curl -sS -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" https://control-plane.redwood.internal/internal/audit-exports/{tenant_id}/status`
  (status shows last_success_ts, last_error, paused=true/false, checkpoint_ts)

Aisha Bello: what about DLQ handling? any gotchas?

Rafael Mendes: DLQ gotchas:
- DLQ depth should be ~0. if it starts growing, do NOT spam replay.
- replay tool (guardrailed) lives in exporter image:
  `audit-exporter replay --tenant <id> --destination splunk --from-dlq --max-batches 50`
- always check downstream health first; replay while Splunk still 429’ing just makes things worse.
- replay will create duplicates; tell customer to dedup by `event_id`.

Sean Gallagher: +1. if DLQ > 1000 batches or growing steadily, page secondary and consider pausing exports to prevent runaway.

Rafael Mendes: When to involve Security (Marcus/Kevin):
- any sign of export sending to an unrecognized host / destination tampering (possible exfil)
- if logs contain unexpected sensitive fields (PII) or raw request payloads (should be redacted)
- if we suspect credentials exposure (Splunk HEC token / Datadog key showing up in logs, even partially)
- if customer asks for compliance language re: retention vs export responsibilities beyond the FAQ

Marcus Lin: +1. also loop us in if you’re asked to widen egress allowlist for a non-standard SIEM endpoint. we need threat-model review.

Rafael Mendes: Notes from today’s incident specifics:
- affected tenants: 2 (beta) using Splunk HEC; both had customer-side limit set lower than expected
- we paused Splunk for tenant A for ~22m, then resumed after customer confirmed HEC throttle settings updated
- no evidence of dropped events in exporter; checkpoints monotonic; DLQ remained at 0
- watch for recurrence around top of hour (their SIEM pipelines seemed to burst)

Aisha Bello: thanks. who is the POC if I need customer comms?

Rafael Mendes: customer comms: route through TAM/CSM; for the two tenants today it was Liam O'Rourke + Miguel Santos. if urgent/outage, use #customer-success + incident channel.

Logan Wright: also FYI we deployed a small config change earlier: reduced default Splunk batch payload size (helps avoid 413/429 cascades). no rollback expected.

Sean Gallagher: alright. Aisha, page me if lag breaches 30m or if auto-pause triggers on more than 3 tenants.

Aisha Bello: ack. I’m on it. :saluting_face:

Rafael Mendes: handing off. I’ll stay available for ~30m if anything flares.

title: #architecture: 1739520203
preview:
Ethan Park: need final eyes on ADR before we mark “ready for approval” today. main open item: make the decision points super explicit + document rejected alternatives (blue/green etc). anyone have last-minute concerns?

Talia Benson: +1 please. we keep getting the same questions in review: “why not blue/green” and “what exactly is rollback guaranteed to do.”

Connor O'Brien: read through the draft this morning. i think we need a crisp “Decision” section w/ bullets:
- in-place upgrade driven by generated upgrade plan artifact
- N-1 -> N only initially
- rollback is best-effort but deterministic within contract (and what’s out of scope)

Sean Gallagher: biggest thing for me is we don’t oversell rollback. should say: rollback is guaranteed to restore control-plane + config state to prior version *if* prechecks pass + backup completed + we haven’t crossed non-reversible migration boundary.

Michael Nguyen: on “non-reversible”: we should be very direct about schema migrations.
- default is backward-compatible migrations (additive) for N-1 -> N
- if we ever need a breaking migration, rollback becomes “restore-from-backup required” and we treat as manual / escalated path

Benji Okafor: +1. also include that we’re not relying on `helm rollback` as the mechanism. helm rollback alone doesn’t revert CRD changes / externalized state.

Rafael Mendes: yep. oncall perspective: we need a simple “rollback entry points” paragraph:
- rollback before CP cutover (safe)
- rollback after CP upgraded but before DP rollout (should be safe)
- rollback after DP changes (depends on migrations + workload)

Ethan Park: makes sense. ADR currently has “phases” but not the explicit contract language.

Quentin Harris: can we add the compatibility policy tie-in? i.e. upgrade plan generation is gated by policy evaluation, blocks unsupported jumps.

Connor O'Brien: yes please. reviewers keep asking “what stops someone from jumping 2 major versions.”

Naomi Feldman: from security side: rejected alternatives should mention why blue/green isn’t automatically “safer” for regulated on-prem. data duplication + two live control planes + key material handling. also cost.

Marcus Lin: also network: blue/green implies DNS / LB cutovers. in private deployments we can’t assume customer has automated traffic shifting, esp on-prem with static VIPs.

Allison Grant: observability note: in-place with plan gives us an auditable step log and we can emit structured events per step. blue/green cutover tends to look like a single flip unless we build extra instrumentation.

Sean Gallagher: also operationally, blue/green doubles failure modes (two clusters to keep healthy, drift, split-brain if control plane talks to wrong dp). i’d love that called out explicitly.

Talia Benson: ok but we should be fair: blue/green *does* reduce blast radius sometimes. so ADR should say “considered, rejected for v1 due to X” not “never.”

Ethan Park: agreed. section: “Rejected alternatives (for v1)” and include a “revisit when we have…” list.

Michael Nguyen: wording suggestion for migrations:
- “We optimize for forward-only additive migrations to preserve rollbackability via versioned deploy rollback + config restore.”
- “When not possible, rollback requires restore-from-backup; installer will surface ‘manual intervention required’ and stop.”

Rafael Mendes: +1 add explicit “stop points.” oncall needs clear criteria: when rollback is recommended vs when to escalate.

Connor O'Brien: also call out why “always rebuild cluster” isn’t viable: on-prem hardware procurement / scheduling + long drain times. plus customers don’t want new cluster IDs/certs every upgrade.

Benji Okafor: don’t forget “blue/green inside same cluster via namespaces” option. we looked at it: still needs CRD versioning, shared DB, shared LB => not actually isolated.

Ethan Park: yep. list of alternatives to capture:
1) full blue/green cluster swap
2) namespace-based blue/green
3) helm-only rollback
4) immutable “recreate everything” (tear down + reinstall)

Sean Gallagher: and for each, one paragraph on why it’s rejected. keep it short.

Quentin Harris: for #1, add: requires duplicate capacity (GPUs esp) + artifact mirrors for both stacks. for VPC customers maybe ok, for on-prem often impossible.

Naomi Feldman: for #3 helm-only rollback: add security angle: doesn’t roll back secrets refs / external KMS config changes, so could leave cluster in inconsistent auth state.

Marcus Lin: for blue/green: mention customer networking constraints: changing endpoint IPs / internal DNS propagation can take minutes-hours. can’t do safe cutover inside the upgrade SLO.

Allison Grant: “decision point” i want in ADR: we store upgrade plan + execution state as an auditable artifact (support bundle + audit log events). that’s a key differentiator vs ad-hoc scripts.

Connor O'Brien: +1 and tie to change-management gating: prechecks must pass, backups must succeed before any irreversible step.

Ethan Park: ok action items i’ll do now:
- add explicit “Decision” bullets
- add “Rollback contract” subsection (guarantees + stop points)
- add “Rejected alternatives (v1)” section w/ blue/green + others
- add “Revisit” criteria (ex: when we have traffic shifting primitives + dual-write migrations)

Sean Gallagher: can you also add a sentence: “rollback is a first-class command that consumes stored state; it is idempotent” ? reviewers keep asking.

Ethan Park: yes.

Talia Benson: timeline-wise, can we get the updated doc in ~1 hr so i can attach to the approval request?

Ethan Park: yep. will post link when updated.

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: also please add a note that N-1 -> N is enforced by policy in the installer, not just “recommended.”

Quentin Harris: :thumbsup: i can review the policy wording once you paste.

Naomi Feldman: :thumbsup: ping me on the security bits.

Ethan Park: updated ADR draft is up w/ new sections. please review: rejected alternatives now includes blue/green + namespace-based + helm-only + recreate. link in drive: https://confluence.redwoodinference.local/x/ADR-Private-Upgrade-Plan-Rollback (same page)

Sean Gallagher: reading now. first pass looks good. “stop points” paragraph is what we needed.

Connor O'Brien: skimmed. +1. small nit: in “Revisit blue/green” add “when we can guarantee deterministic CP<->DP pairing during cutover” (avoid split brain).

Ethan Park: added.

Allison Grant: love the auditability callout. also added note re: emitting audit events per phase.

Talia Benson: looks good to me. moving to approval request w/ SRE + Release Eng.

Ethan Park: thanks all. marking ADR “final review complete” unless more comments come in.

System message: Reactions — Connor O'Brien added :white_check_mark:, Sean Gallagher added :thumbsup:, Naomi Feldman added :lock:, Rafael Mendes added :thumbsup:

- default is backward-compatible migrations (additive) for N-1 -> N
- if we ever need a breaking migration, rollback becomes “restore-from-backup required” and we treat as manual / escalated path

Benji Okafor: +1. also include that we’re not relying on `helm rollback` as the mechanism. helm rollback alone doesn’t revert CRD changes / externalized state.

Rafael Mendes: yep. oncall perspective: we need a simple “rollback entry points” paragraph:
- rollback before CP cutover (safe)
- rollback after CP upgraded but before DP rollout (should be safe)
- rollback after DP changes (depends on migrations + workload)

Ethan Park: makes sense. ADR currently has “phases” but not the explicit contract language.

Quentin Harris: can we add the compatibility policy tie-in? i.e. upgrade plan generation is gated by policy evaluation, blocks unsupported jumps.

Connor O'Brien: yes please. reviewers keep asking “what stops someone from jumping 2 major versions.”

Naomi Feldman: from security side: rejected alternatives should mention why blue/green isn’t automatically “safer” for regulated on-prem. data duplication + two live control planes + key material handling. also cost.

Marcus Lin: also network: blue/green implies DNS / LB cutovers. in private deployments we can’t assume customer has automated traffic shifting, esp on-prem with static VIPs.

Allison Grant: observability note: in-place with plan gives us an auditable step log and we can emit structured events per step. blue/green cutover tends to look like a single flip unless we build extra instrumentation.

Sean Gallagher: also operationally, blue/green doubles failure modes (two clusters to keep healthy, drift, split-brain if control plane talks to wrong dp). i’d love that called out explicitly.

Talia Benson: ok but we should be fair: blue/green *does* reduce blast radius sometimes. so ADR should say “considered, rejected for v1 due to X” not “never.”

Ethan Park: agreed. section: “Rejected alternatives (for v1)” and include a “revisit when we have…” list.

Michael Nguyen: wording suggestion for migrations:
- “We optimize for forward-only additive migrations to preserve rollbackability via versioned deploy rollback + config restore.”
- “When not possible, rollback requires restore-from-backup; installer will surface ‘manual intervention required’ and stop.”

Rafael Mendes: +1 add explicit “stop points.” oncall needs clear criteria: when rollback is recommended vs when to escalate.

Connor O'Brien: also call out why “always rebuild cluster” isn’t viable: on-prem hardware procurement / scheduling + long drain times. plus customers don’t want new cluster IDs/certs every upgrade.

Benji Okafor: don’t forget “blue/green inside same cluster via namespaces” option. we looked at it: still needs CRD versioning, shared DB, shared LB => not actually isolated.

Ethan Park: yep. list of alternatives to capture:
1) full blue/green cluster swap
2) namespace-based blue/green
3) helm-only rollback
4) immutable “recreate everything” (tear down + reinstall)

Sean Gallagher: and for each, one paragraph on why it’s rejected. keep it short.

Quentin Harris: for #1, add: requires duplicate capacity (GPUs esp) + artifact mirrors for both stacks. for VPC customers maybe ok, for on-prem often impossible.

Naomi Feldman: for #3 helm-only rollback: add security angle: doesn’t roll back secrets refs / external KMS config changes, so could leave cluster in inconsistent auth state.

Marcus Lin: for blue/green: mention customer networking constraints: changing endpoint IPs / internal DNS propagation can take minutes-hours. can’t do safe cutover inside the upgrade SLO.

Allison Grant: “decision point” i want in ADR: we store upgrade plan + execution state as an auditable artifact (support bundle + audit log events). that’s a key differentiator vs ad-hoc scripts.

Connor O'Brien: +1 and tie to change-management gating: prechecks must pass, backups must succeed before any irreversible step.

Ethan Park: ok action items i’ll do now:
- add explicit “Decision” bullets
- add “Rollback contract” subsection (guarantees + stop points)
- add “Rejected alternatives (v1)” section w/ blue/green + others
- add “Revisit” criteria (ex: when we have traffic shifting primitives + dual-write migrations)

Sean Gallagher: can you also add a sentence: “rollback is a first-class command that consumes stored state; it is idempotent” ? reviewers keep asking.

Ethan Park: yes.

Talia Benson: timeline-wise, can we get the updated doc in ~1 hr so i can attach to the approval request?

Ethan Park: yep. will post link when updated.

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: also please add a note that N-1 -> N is enforced by policy in the installer, not just “recommended.”

Quentin Harris: :thumbsup: i can review the policy wording once you paste.

Naomi Feldman: :thumbsup: ping me on the security bits.

Ethan Park: updated ADR draft is up w/ new sections. please review: rejected alternatives now includes blue/green + namespace-based + helm-only + recreate. link in drive: https://confluence.redwoodinference.local/x/ADR-Private-Upgrade-Plan-Rollback (same page)

Sean Gallagher: reading now. first pass looks good. “stop points” paragraph is what we needed.

Connor O'Brien: skimmed. +1. small nit: in “Revisit blue/green” add “when we can guarantee deterministic CP<->DP pairing during cutover” (avoid split brain).

Ethan Park: added.

Allison Grant: love the auditability callout. also added note re: emitting audit events per phase.

Talia Benson: looks good to me. moving to approval request w/ SRE + Release Eng.

Ethan Park: thanks all. marking ADR “final review complete” unless more comments come in.

System message: Reactions — Connor O'Brien added :white_check_mark:, Sean Gallagher added :thumbsup:, Naomi Feldman added :lock:, Rafael Mendes added :thumbsup:

title: #eng-infra: 1739511102
preview:
Sahana Iyer: seeing a bunch of CI flakes on the `private-upgrade-e2e` suite again. last 10 nightly runs: 4 failures, and at least 3 look non-deterministic (rerun passed). this is the ENG-4877 track but it’s now blocking a couple PRs due to required checks.

Sahana Iyer: failure modes from the last two:
- k8s cluster never reaches ready (kind) within 8m, then everything cascades
- snapshot hook step hangs (CSI) and times out
- intermittent `helm upgrade` failing w/ `context deadline exceeded` when waiting on pre-upgrade job

Sahana Iyer: question: do we add retries (rerun jobs / rerun steps) or should we invest in more deterministic fixtures first? trying to pick the right “stop the bleeding” move.

github-actions[bot]: Workflow run failed: private-upgrade-e2e / scenario=onprem-offline-bundle
Logs: https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-private-installer/actions/runs/93218810

Devon Price: +1 we should stop the bleeding, but blanket retries make the signal worse.

Devon Price: can we do targeted retries based on error signatures? ex: retry only if we see `failed to pull image` / `TLS handshake timeout` / `apiserver not ready`.

Devon Price: we can also add `--atomic=false` to avoid helm auto-rollback masking what actually happened (then we can still clean up in teardown). also please attach `kubectl describe pod` + events as artifacts.

Leila Farouk: my vote: deterministic fixtures first, but agree we need a short-term mitigation.

Leila Farouk: a lot of our “e2e” is accidentally testing the internet + docker hub + runner variance:
- images aren’t pinned to digests everywhere
- we don’t pre-pull images, so a slow pull trips timeouts
- DB seed/migration duration varies depending on node load

Leila Farouk: if we can:
1) pin image digests for the test environment
2) pre-pull into the cluster (or local registry mirror)
3) set fixed resource requests/limits in the test charts
…we’ll cut 80% of the flakes.

Irene Choi: infra context: shared GH runners are a big part of this. we see CPU steal + network egress throttling during US morning hours.

Irene Choi: the `kind` “never ready” is usually just the node container starting slowly. we can add a readiness probe that fails fast with a clear error *and* bump the timeout (8m is tight when the runner is slow).

Irene Choi: medium term: I can help get a small dedicated runner pool for these upgrade e2e jobs (or move the nightly to our internal runners). but that won’t land this week.

Rafael Mendes: +1 on not turning retries into a broom.

Rafael Mendes: for release gating, we need to be able to say “this passed once” actually means something. otherwise we’ll start shipping regressions and blaming CI.

Rafael Mendes: suggestion:
- classify flakes into known transient buckets
- only auto-retry those buckets (max 1 retry)
- anything else is a hard fail

Rafael Mendes: also, snapshot hook timeouts might be real: the CSI backend in tests is sometimes not actually supporting what our hook expects. are we running hostpath in kind for the storage path that should be “CSI”? if yes, that’s basically random.

Sahana Iyer: re snapshot: current fixture uses hostpath + a fake snapshot controller in kind. it’s… fragile. we did it to avoid needing real cloud storage in CI.

Leila Farouk: that explains a lot. fake snapshot controllers are notorious.

Leila Farouk: could we split the matrix?
- PR gating: logical backup path only (no snapshot), plus rollback state machine checks
- nightly: include snapshot path tests (and accept higher runtime)

Connor O'Brien: jumping in — for Private release gating we should not rely on a flaky scenario.

Connor O'Brien: +1 to splitting. PR gating should cover N-1 → N happy path with deterministic infra. snapshot coverage can be nightly + pre-release.

Connor O'Brien: for the short-term, I’m okay with 1 retry at the *job* level for known infra flakes, but we need to log when it happened and keep a metric (“flake rate”) so it doesn’t become permanent.

Devon Price: we can implement “known infra flake” detection pretty quickly:
- parse the junit + job logs for strings
- if match, rerun job once and mark the PR check as `passed_with_retry` (or at least annotate in summary)

Devon Price: GH Actions doesn’t have a first-class “passed with retry”, but we can print it in the check summary + emit a metric.

Irene Choi: on timeouts: the helm wait failure (`context deadline exceeded`) might just be a bad timeout default in the pre-upgrade hook job. we recently added more prechecks + artifact verification, which increases that job’s runtime.

Irene Choi: can we bump the hook timeout in the test values file, and separately keep prod defaults conservative?

Sahana Iyer: makes sense. i can bump in fixture values for e2e. will keep prod values unchanged.

Rafael Mendes: also add better debug on failure:
- `kubectl get jobs -A`
- `kubectl logs job/<preupgrade> -n <ns> --tail=-1`
- `kubectl get events -A --sort-by=.lastTimestamp | tail -200`

Rafael Mendes: half the “flake” is just “we don’t know why it failed.”

Leila Farouk: +1. and for the determinism work, I can take the “pin digests + pre-pull” action item.

Leila Farouk: we should also freeze the test bundle inputs. i saw a couple runs pull latest from the artifact mirror, which is… not ideal for a reproducible upgrade test.

Sahana Iyer: action plan proposal:
1) immediate: add 1 auto-retry ONLY for known infra flakes (apiserver/kind ready, image pull/TLS timeouts). Devon, can you help with the log signature list?
2) split matrix: PR gating uses logical backup path only; snapshot path nightly/pre-release. Connor ok?
3) improve fixtures: pin image digests + pre-pull; freeze artifact bundle versions.
4) increase e2e-only timeout for the helm pre-upgrade job + add better artifact collection on failure.

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: for #2. PR gating should be stable + representative. snapshot path nightly is fine.

Devon Price: yep, i’ll draft the signature list and wire the rerun (max 1). will drop it behind a flag so we can disable quickly.

Irene Choi: i’ll look into runner pool options + whether we can run nightly on internal runners (and what it costs). but that’s separate from the immediate fixes.

Leila Farouk: taking #3. will open a ticket + PR for digest pinning + pre-pull init job in the test chart.

Sahana Iyer: awesome. i’ll do #4 today and update ENG-4877 + INT-3302 with these decisions. thanks all.

title: #eng-infra: 1739399020
preview:
Gabe Wilson: seeing NCCL post-provision smoke test flaking again in us-east A100. validation agent marks node as failed -> orchestrator retries -> sometimes passes on 2nd attempt. anyone else looking at this?

Gabe Wilson: log excerpt from one of the failures (from /var/log/redwood/validation/nccl-smoke.log):
```bash
+ timeout 90s bash -lc 'NCCL_DEBUG=INFO NCCL_SOCKET_IFNAME=eth0 ./all_reduce_perf -b 8M -e 256M -f 2 -g 8'
NCCL version 2.18.3+cuda12.1
...
WARN NET/Socket : Connection closed by remote peer
INFO transport/net_socket.cc:67 -> 2
WARN init.cc:868 -> 2
ncclInternalError: Internal check failed.
Last error:
Error: failed to connect to 10.42.18.93<32415>
```

Vanessa Ortiz: 90s seems tight if the node is still warming up / ib devices settling. what’s the pass latency when it succeeds?

Gabe Wilson: on success, the test itself is like ~18-30s. but we have a few that take 70-85s end-to-end (incl pulling image + init). failures tend to be right around the timeout.

Nina Petrova: “connection closed by remote peer” smells like fabric not ready or transient kube networking / CNI not settled. are we running this immediately after kubelet join?

Gabe Wilson: yep. flow is: JoinCluster -> LabelNode -> Deploy validation DaemonSet -> run nccl check.

Keiko Yamamoto: if it’s right after join, node can still be in the window where routes / iptables rules are being applied. can we add backoff between join and nccl smoke?

Harish Reddy: +1. ops perspective: we’d rather add 2 mins to provisioning than quarantine a good node and then reimage.

Sahana Iyer: we also saw occasional image pull slowness on first boot (containerd doing snapshotter work). that can eat into the timeout.

Miles Thornton: what’s the observed flake rate? if it’s a couple % we should treat as retryable with structured telemetry, not quarantine-worthy on first attempt.

Gabe Wilson: from last 24h in the staged rollout pool: 14 failures / 312 nodes attempted (~4.5%). of those, 9 passed on immediate retry, 3 passed after re-running validations manually, 2 quarantined.

Aisha Bello: if we’re going to retry, please ensure metrics don’t explode cardinality-wise. tag by step + failure class, not node id. (we already got burned once :))

Rafael Mendes: SRE side: we can tolerate a retry as long as we cap total time-to-ready. agree quarantine should be after repeated failures + evidence it’s not just timing.

Vanessa Ortiz: propose:
1) increase nccl smoke timeout to 240s
2) add explicit pre-check that the validation pod has been running for N seconds and has pulled image already
3) retry policy: 3 attempts, exponential backoff (30s, 60s, 120s) before marking step failed
4) only quarantine after nccl fails on 3 attempts (and only if error matches “connection closed/timeout” bucket?)

Nina Petrova: careful on quarantining based solely on that string; we should split error classes:
- timeout (test didn’t finish)
- socket connect failures
- NCCL errors w/ Xid / nvlink (hardware)
Only the last category should auto-quarantine quickly.

Gabe Wilson: good point. another snippet from a different node (this one *should* quarantine):
```bash
NCCL WARN Call to connect returned Connection timed out, retrying
NCCL WARN NET/Socket : call to connect returned Connection refused
...
CUDA failure 'an illegal memory access was encountered'
NCCL WARN Cuda failure 'an illegal memory access was encountered'
```

Sahana Iyer: illegal mem access -> likely bad GPU / driver issue. but the earlier “remote peer” could just be timing.

Keiko Yamamoto: can we gate nccl smoke test on a simple “network ready” check first? like ping to peer pods or verify node has expected routes? even a tiny TCP check between pods could reduce false negatives.

Gabe Wilson: we already do a basic DNS + outbound check. we don’t do pod-to-pod. could add a lightweight “mesh handshake” between the nccl pods before running all_reduce.

Sergio Costa: if you add handshake, instrument it separately so we can see which sub-step is failing (handshake vs nccl). otherwise dashboards just show “nccl failed” and it’s opaque.

Aisha Bello: +1. also keep span names stable. something like:
- provision.validation.nccl.handshake
- provision.validation.nccl.all_reduce

Vanessa Ortiz: action items:
- Gabe: update validation agent to (a) longer timeout, (b) retries/backoff, (c) classify errors into buckets. link it to ENG-4824 + ENG-4825.
- Keiko: propose where to insert delay between join and validation start (or check “node network ready”)
- Sahana: confirm containerd image pull time distribution; if needed we pre-pull nccl image in the golden image build or early daemon.

Gabe Wilson: ack. I’ll draft changes behind a config flag so we can tune without redeploy. probably values:
- `nccl.timeoutSeconds`: 240
- `nccl.maxAttempts`: 3
- `nccl.backoffSeconds`: 30 (exp)
- `nccl.quarantineOn`: `hardware_error_only`

Rafael Mendes: please ensure quarantine policy change is consistent with the post-provision quarantine draft. we don’t want agents making unilateral quarantine decisions without emitting the right reason.

Gabe Wilson: yep, quarantine will still be orchestrator decision; agent just returns `failure_class=transient_network|timeout|hardware` + log pointer.

Miles Thornton: also: include the observed attempt count/duration in the validation artifact so we can compute “would have passed if we waited longer”.

Gabe Wilson: will add fields: `attempt`, `elapsed_ms`, `exit_code`, `failure_class`, `last_20_lines`.

Vanessa Ortiz: cool. let’s try this in us-east A100 only. success criteria: nccl flake <1% and no big hit to P95 time-to-ready.

Aisha Bello: I’ll watch the dashboard once the change lands. ping me when the metric names are finalized so I can ensure alerts don’t page on the transient bucket during rollout.

Sergio Costa: +1, share the metric names. if you’re adding `failure_class`, that’s a good dimension.

Gabe Wilson: will do. PR later today.

Harish Reddy: from ops: if nodes are stuck in Validating longer due to retries, can we surface “attempt 2/3 (backing off 60s)” somewhere? makes oncall less nervous.

Gabe Wilson: good call. readiness service already supports “details” string; we can populate with current attempt/backoff.

Vanessa Ortiz: ok let’s do it. thanks all.

- `nccl.maxAttempts`: 3
- `nccl.backoffSeconds`: 30 (exp)
- `nccl.quarantineOn`: `hardware_error_only`

Rafael Mendes: please ensure quarantine policy change is consistent with the post-provision quarantine draft. we don’t want agents making unilateral quarantine decisions without emitting the right reason.

Gabe Wilson: yep, quarantine will still be orchestrator decision; agent just returns `failure_class=transient_network|timeout|hardware` + log pointer.

Miles Thornton: also: include the observed attempt count/duration in the validation artifact so we can compute “would have passed if we waited longer”.

Gabe Wilson: will add fields: `attempt`, `elapsed_ms`, `exit_code`, `failure_class`, `last_20_lines`.

Vanessa Ortiz: cool. let’s try this in us-east A100 only. success criteria: nccl flake <1% and no big hit to P95 time-to-ready.

Aisha Bello: I’ll watch the dashboard once the change lands. ping me when the metric names are finalized so I can ensure alerts don’t page on the transient bucket during rollout.

Sergio Costa: +1, share the metric names. if you’re adding `failure_class`, that’s a good dimension.

Gabe Wilson: will do. PR later today.

Harish Reddy: from ops: if nodes are stuck in Validating longer due to retries, can we surface “attempt 2/3 (backing off 60s)” somewhere? makes oncall less nervous.

Gabe Wilson: good call. readiness service already supports “details” string; we can populate with current attempt/backoff.

Vanessa Ortiz: ok let’s do it. thanks all.

title: #architecture: 1739401180
preview:
Ethan Park: pulling folks into this thread from the installer workstream — we need to land rollback semantics + schema migration strategy this week so Connor can cut the next Private train. current ADR draft is here: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/adr-private-upgrade-plan-and-rollback-contract

Ethan Park: main open question: what do we *guarantee* on rollback when migrations have run + pods are partially updated? i want something deterministic and enforceable by the installer.

Michael Nguyen: +1. the hard part is DB schema. if we do irreversible migrations (drops, data backfills that change meaning), rollback becomes “restore from backup” only.

Benji Okafor: installer/state machine can do “rollback hooks” but it can’t invent down-migrations. we need to decide contract: either (a) only expand/contract migrations w/ backward compatibility window, or (b) rollback = restore snapshot and accept data loss since last backup.

Connor O'Brien: for release eng, i need a crisp line for GA: is rollback always possible via installer command? or “best-effort depending on migration class”? because that changes gating + comms.

Sean Gallagher: from SRE POV: we need to reduce the class of rollbacks that turn into 2am archaeology. if it’s “restore snapshot” only, we *must* enforce pre-upgrade backup, and we should assume some downtime during restore.

Ethan Park: right. what i’m leaning toward: in-place upgrade, rollback is supported if:
1) upgrade didn’t reach “commit point” (data plane pinned to old, CP not upgraded), OR
2) after commit, rollback requires backup restore for DB + config state

Michael Nguyen: can we define “commit point” as “schema migrations applied”? that’s clean. before migrations: revert is essentially helm rollback + installer state rewind.

Benji Okafor: careful: CP deploy can happen before migrations (depending on chart ordering). also we have config persistence changes in CP that might write new fields.

Quentin Harris: compatibility matrix perspective: if we require expand/contract, we can keep CP N compatible w/ DB schema N-1 (and DP can lag too). but that’s an explicit policy we need to enforce in CI.

Connor O'Brien: yeah, but “expand/contract for everything” is a big dev tax. what’s the minimal bar for v1? we scoped N-1 -> N only.

Rafael Mendes: also partial upgrades are the real failure mode in the wild. e.g., chart upgrade applied, some deployments updated, then migration job OOMs, and now the system is half new/half old.

Sean Gallagher: ^ exactly. rollback command needs to handle “partial chart rollout” + “partial migration.” idempotency matters.

Michael Nguyen: for DB migrations: we can enforce:
- migrations must be idempotent
- lock timeout defaults (so we fail fast rather than wedge)
- a pre-migration “schema guard” that blocks if current version != expected (prevents skipping)

Michael Nguyen: but that doesn’t solve down-migration. i recommend we *don’t* promise down-migrations. we promise either:
A) rollback without restore only if migrations are backward compatible, or
B) rollback via restore (snapshot) always.

Allison Grant: auditability angle: whatever we decide, we need to emit in audit logs: did we run migrations? did we hit commit? did we restore? and attach backup id + plan id.

Naomi Feldman: security/compliance POV: restore-based rollback is acceptable if it’s explicit and we have evidence (who triggered, what snapshot, encryption/KMS). but the doc must clearly call out potential data loss window.

Ethan Park: what about “dual write” / “write both schemas” as a pattern? seems heavy.

Michael Nguyen: dual-write is overkill for v1. the practical pattern is expand/contract:
- N migration: add columns/tables, don’t remove
- app code can read old + new
- contract (drop) only in N+1 or later

Benji Okafor: expand/contract is feasible for most config tables + control-plane metadata. but there are some “breaking” changes we’ve wanted (e.g., normalizing request logs). those would need a different path.

Connor O'Brien: we can set policy:
- default: expand/contract required for any schema touched during a release train that wants “helm rollback only”
- exception: if you need breaking migration, you must require backup restore rollback (block in release checklist unless docs updated + approval)

Sean Gallagher: + add an explicit “rollback mode” in the upgrade plan output:
- rollback_mode=\"in_place\" if no breaking migrations
- rollback_mode=\"restore_required\" if breaking migrations

Sean Gallagher: and the installer should surface this BEFORE upgrade start. no surprises mid-flight.

Ethan Park: love that. can we make it mechanized? e.g., migration tool emits metadata “reversible=false” or “requires_restore=true”.

Michael Nguyen: we can tag migrations with something like:
-- requires_restore: true
-- compatible_with_prev: true/false
and the upgrader can refuse unless backup completed.

Quentin Harris: plus version gating: if rollback_mode=restore_required then only allow N-1 -> N with a known backup provider (EBS/CSI/logical) configured.

Benji Okafor: installer already has prechecks framework now (pluggable). we can add a blocking check: “backup target configured + writable + encryption configured” when restore_required.

Connor O'Brien: ok but we also need to settle “partial upgrade” semantics. how do we detect what applied? helm history is not enough; jobs can succeed/fail.

Benji Okafor: proposal:
- installer writes an “upgrade state” object (local + persisted in cluster secret/CRD)
- each stage sets checkpoints: artifacts pulled, prechecks passed, backup done, CP upgraded, migrations run, DP upgraded
- rollback command consults state and runs the inverse plan for completed checkpoints.

Rafael Mendes: + make rollback idempotent. if you run rollback twice, it shouldn’t make things worse.

Michael Nguyen: for DB:
- if migrations didn’t start: do nothing
- if migrations started but not marked complete: assume unknown, force restore (because partial migration is the worst state)
- if migrations complete and compatible_with_prev=true: you *may* do in-place rollback (apps old version can run)
- if migrations complete and requires_restore=true: force restore

Sean Gallagher: yes. we should never try to “continue migration backward.” just restore.

Allison Grant: also need to define post-rollback verification: cluster health checks, DB schema version check, and a small control-plane readiness probe. and log them.

Naomi Feldman: please explicitly document what’s included in backup (db + config + secret refs) and what’s not (model cache, ephemeral logs). compliance customers will ask.

Ethan Park: noted. the backup doc covers most of that but we’ll align language.

Confluence Bot: Ethan Park shared a link: adr-private-upgrade-plan-and-rollback-contract (draft)

Connor O'Brien: action items i’m hearing:
1) Ethan/Michael update ADR with commit point definition + rollback modes
2) Benji add plan field rollback_mode + installer precheck to require backup when restore_required
3) Sean/Rafael provide SRE wording for “partial migration => force restore”

Sean Gallagher: yep. and add approval gate: any release w/ requires_restore migrations needs SRE + Release Eng signoff (ties to change management doc).

Quentin Harris: i’ll add a note to compatibility policy doc: “N control plane must be able to run against schema N-1 unless migration is flagged requires_restore.”

Michael Nguyen: i’ll draft migration tagging conventions today and post in #eng-platform. (reaction :thumbsup: from Ethan Park)

Ethan Park: cool. i’ll update ADR by EOD and @connor_obrien for review. goal is to stop bikeshedding in PR comments.

Connor O'Brien: :+1: once ADR is updated i’ll reference it in the release checklist and the PR template for migrations.

Rafael Mendes: one more: we should explicitly say model cache is not rolled back and may be cleared during rollback. ops needs to expect warmup latency after restore.

Sean Gallagher: +1, and runbook should include “post-rollback warmup + verify canary query.”

Ethan Park: agreed. capturing in ADR under “non-goals / expected side effects.”

title: #eng-infra: 1739390021
preview:
Irene Choi: pulling folks into this thread re backup/snapshot mechanism for Private upgrades. need something that works across AWS EBS, generic CSI (EKS + on-prem), and fallback to logical backups. what’s the realistic lowest common denom?

Vanessa Ortiz: for VPC installs on AWS we can do EBS snapshots pretty reliably, but if PV is not EBS (gp3) then we’re back in CSI land. also: data plane caches are “nice to have” restore, control plane DB is “must be consistent”.

Fatima Noor: on-prem: no EBS, obviously. and not every customer cluster has CSI snapshot CRDs installed/enabled. some are on Ceph/Rook (supports snapshots), some on vSphere (depends), some on bare metal NFS (no snapshots). anything that *requires* VolumeSnapshot will be a blocker.

Bruno Silva: +1. I’ve seen air-gapped customers with storageclass = nfs-client provisioner. there is no snapshot capability. also no object store sometimes, just local S3-compatible minio.

Sean Gallagher: from SRE side: during upgrade we need “backup succeeded” to mean we can actually roll back without guesswork. if the backup is best-effort, we should label it WARN and require operator acknowledgement.

Irene Choi: current proposal in backup strategy doc is:
- primary: DB logical backup (`pg_dump` / `pg_dumpall` depending) + config/state export
- optional: PV snapshot where supported (EBS snapshot or CSI VolumeSnapshot)
- optional: model cache snapshot (can be skipped)
Question is: do we treat PV snapshots as REQUIRED for rollback guarantee?

Rafael Mendes: I’d avoid “required” unless we can detect support and provide fallback. rollback should work even if only DB/config backed up. caches can be repopulated.

Vanessa Ortiz: agree. but we should be explicit what PVs we care about. control plane DB volume is biggest one. if DB is external (RDS) then snapshots are a different API anyway.

Bruno Silva: installer can detect topology:
- embedded postgres (statefulset + PVC) -> do pg_dump + (if available) volume snapshot
- external postgres (customer managed) -> require credentials + we can do pg_dump OR they provide a backup hook (we just verify last backup timestamp?)

Connor O'Brien: release eng perspective: need deterministic behavior in upgrade plan. I’m nervous about “try snapshot if possible” because it changes rollback semantics run-to-run. can we encode snapshot strategy in plan output?

Irene Choi: yes. plan generator can output something like:
- backup_steps: ["preflight.storage.snapshot_api_available", "db.logical_dump", "pvc.snapshot (skipped: not_supported)"]
so it’s auditable.

Devon Price: if we’re using CSI snapshots, we should standardize on K8s VolumeSnapshot API. but we need preflight that checks:
- CRDs exist
- at least 1 VolumeSnapshotClass
- the PVC storageclass supports it (not always easy)

Sahana Iyer: for tests: we can cover EBS snapshots in AWS ephemeral clusters, and CSI snapshots via a test driver (maybe hostpath csi w/ snapshot support). but NFS no-snapshot path should also be validated (logical only).

Fatima Noor: on-prem constraint: many clusters are locked down; installing snapshot-controller CRDs is a change request. we should *not* require that to upgrade. if present, great.

Sean Gallagher: also: time. snapshots are fast but `pg_dump` on large DB can be slow; we need to set expectations and timeouts. if pg_dump fails, that should block upgrade.

Vanessa Ortiz: + remind: consistent backup needs app quiesce. at least: put control plane into “maintenance mode” / stop writes before snapshot/dump.

Irene Choi: yep. sequencing we’ve been using:
1) prechecks
2) pause reconcilers / maintenance mode
3) backup (db dump + optional snapshots)
4) upgrade control plane
5) run migrations
6) upgrade data plane
7) resume reconcilers

Bruno Silva: how do we implement EBS snapshot? call AWS API directly from installer? that gets gnarly w/ IAM.

Vanessa Ortiz: for EKS we can rely on CSI snapshot (ebs-csi supports it). better than bespoke AWS API in installer.

Fatima Noor: but note: some customers run older EKS and don’t have snapshot CRDs installed even though EBS CSI driver supports it.

Devon Price: preflight can detect. something like:
`kubectl get crd volumesnapshots.snapshot.storage.k8s.io` and `kubectl get volumesnapshotclass`
If missing -> mark snapshot path unavailable.

Rafael Mendes: what about “filesystem-level” backup? (rsync) probably not reliable/too slow.

Irene Choi: not planning filesystem copy. we’ll keep it to logical dump + snapshot API.

Sean Gallagher: rollback semantics: if only logical DB dump exists, restore is “restore DB + redeploy previous version”. if we also had PVC snapshot, restore is “restore PVC + redeploy previous version”. we need clear precedence.

Irene Choi: makes sense: prefer volume snapshot restore if present (faster) *but* only if taken after maintenance mode. else we fall back to logical restore.

Connor O'Brien: can we document the rule: "Snapshots are opportunistic accelerators, not the source of truth"? so rollback guarantee comes from logical backup.

Fatima Noor: +1. that’s the only story that works on NFS.

Sahana Iyer: can we make preflight print a one-liner summary like:
- DB backup: logical dump (required) -> OK
- Volume snapshots: not supported (warn)
so operators know.

Irene Choi: yup. Also: we should capture in the upgrade report artifact.

Bruno Silva: one more thing: where do we store logical dumps for air-gapped? local path + they can move it?

Fatima Noor: many on-prem want dump stored on an internal S3 (minio) or shared filesystem. installer should support `--backup-target` (s3 endpoint + creds) OR local dir.

Sean Gallagher: from reliability: please default to “backup location must be on durable storage”. local node path is risky if pod dies.

Vanessa Ortiz: + if we’re in k8s job, local path is on container FS unless we mount something. so local mode should require a mounted PVC or hostPath (hostPath is also meh).

Irene Choi: action items:
- add preflight checks: snapshot CRDs/classes present; warn if absent
- backup strategy: logical DB dump is REQUIRED; CSI snapshots optional
- on-prem: support S3-compatible endpoint + local mounted PVC target
- update plan schema to record which snapshot method used / skipped

Devon Price: I’ll add to installer preflight framework: `storage.snapshot_api` check + remediation text (install snapshot-controller, create VolumeSnapshotClass).

Connor O'Brien: I’ll note in release gating checklist that upgrade plan must include backup strategy summary.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: also please ensure we emit audit log event with backup mode (logical only vs logical+snapshot).

Irene Choi: yep, tying into the audit events work. will loop in Allison later.

Sahana Iyer: for CI, I’ll add a scenario: "no snapshot CRDs" -> upgrade proceeds with logical backup only, and rollback uses logical restore.

Fatima Noor: thanks. also please avoid wording like “snapshot required” in operator docs. it’ll trigger a bunch of change-control tickets.

Irene Choi: noted. I’ll update the runbook draft language. closing this thread for now; will link the updated confluence section once it’s in.

Vanessa Ortiz: + if we’re in k8s job, local path is on container FS unless we mount something. so local mode should require a mounted PVC or hostPath (hostPath is also meh).

Irene Choi: action items:
- add preflight checks: snapshot CRDs/classes present; warn if absent
- backup strategy: logical DB dump is REQUIRED; CSI snapshots optional
- on-prem: support S3-compatible endpoint + local mounted PVC target
- update plan schema to record which snapshot method used / skipped

Devon Price: I’ll add to installer preflight framework: `storage.snapshot_api` check + remediation text (install snapshot-controller, create VolumeSnapshotClass).

Connor O'Brien: I’ll note in release gating checklist that upgrade plan must include backup strategy summary.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: also please ensure we emit audit log event with backup mode (logical only vs logical+snapshot).

Irene Choi: yep, tying into the audit events work. will loop in Allison later.

Sahana Iyer: for CI, I’ll add a scenario: "no snapshot CRDs" -> upgrade proceeds with logical backup only, and rollback uses logical restore.

Fatima Noor: thanks. also please avoid wording like “snapshot required” in operator docs. it’ll trigger a bunch of change-control tickets.

Irene Choi: noted. I’ll update the runbook draft language. closing this thread for now; will link the updated confluence section once it’s in.

title: #eng-sre: 1739381107
preview:
Aisha Bello: trying to nail down which saturation signals the bot should *highlight* vs just attach as links. GPU util is noisy. queue depth seems actionable. KV cache pressure is… maybe. thoughts?

Logan Wright: +1 util alone is misleading. 95% util might be “healthy” if batching is doing its thing.
Logan Wright: what’s actionable is *util + symptoms* (p95 latency burn / 5xx / streaming disconnects) and a second saturation indicator.

Jacob Stein: agree. on the incidents I’ve worked, queue depth / scheduler backpressure was the “oh crap” signal.
Jacob Stein: GPU util is like… always 70-98% during busy hours.

Rafael Mendes: can we define “actionable” as: when it changes what we do in first 5 min? (reroute, shed load, scale, revert deploy)

Miles Thornton: for bot v2 we need simple heuristics. if we over-highlight, oncall ignores the card.

Allison Grant: we should also align w alert tuning. we already page on some of these; bot highlights should map to “this is the likely cause”.

Aisha Bello: ok, proposing categories:
- Highlight: queue depth/backpressure, admission control rejects, KV cache evictions (maybe)
- Mention but don’t highlight: GPU util, HBM/mem util
- Deep link only: per-GPU breakdowns, kernel-level stuff

Nadia Rahman: +1. also: be careful with metric definitions across hosted vs dedicated pools.
Nadia Rahman: for hosted, queue depth is at scheduler. for dedicated, some customers run a different batching config and queue depth behaves differently.

Logan Wright: metric candidates we can standardize:
- scheduler_queue_depth (p95 over 5m)
- scheduler_backpressure_ratio (time spent throttling / total)
- admission_control_reject_rate (per sec)
- gpu_sm_utilization (avg over 1m)
- kv_cache_evictions_rate OR kv_cache_pressure (whatever runtime exports)

Caleb Johnson: KV cache “pressure” is tricky. Some runtimes report it as a gauge but semantics differ.
Caleb Johnson: evictions rate is more actionable than “% used”. % used is always ~near cap by design.

Mina Haddad: from routing perspective, if bot highlights “regional saturation” we want it to suggest: route to alt region / enable fallback model variant / reduce max_tokens.
Mina Haddad: queue depth + backpressure is the cleanest input to that decision.

Ravi Menon: +1, and the degraded mode playbook basically keys off “are we queued + throttling” vs “are we failing for other reasons”.
Ravi Menon: GPU util doesn’t tell you if you’re queuing, it just tells you you’re busy.

Aisha Bello: thresholds? need something that triggers the yellow/red callouts in the card.

Allison Grant: current alert draft (not final):
- queue_depth_p95 > 2x baseline for 10m OR absolute > N (depends on pool)
- backpressure_ratio > 0.2 for 5m
- admission_control_reject_rate > 1% of requests for 5m
- kv_cache_evictions_rate elevated + latency burn

Jacob Stein: absolute queue depth thresholds are hard bc RPS varies. can we do time-in-queue instead?
Jacob Stein: e.g. “queue_wait_p95 > 200ms for 5m” would be more portable.

Logan Wright: we have queue wait time but it’s not consistently instrumented in dedicated yet.
Logan Wright: we can do both:
- if queue_wait_p95 exists: use that
- else fallback to queue_depth normalized by capacity (queue_depth / admitted_rps or / gpu_count)

Miles Thornton: bot logic needs to be deterministic + cheap. we can’t be computing baselines on the fly.
Miles Thornton: maybe thresholds are “static per pool tier” and we tune later.

Nadia Rahman: we can ship “v0 thresholds” with conservative highlights:
- RED: backpressure_ratio >= 0.35 OR admission rejects >= 3%
- YELLOW: backpressure_ratio 0.2-0.35 OR admission rejects 1-3%
- queue_depth used as supporting context, not primary highlight

Aisha Bello: what about GPU util? we still include it, but don’t highlight unless it’s low (to rule out saturation?)

Caleb Johnson: interesting angle: low GPU util + high queue depth = likely scheduler issue or upstream throttling / misconfig.
Caleb Johnson: but that’s an edge case. don’t bake too much magic.

Rafael Mendes: for first 5 min, simplest “if backpressure + rejects are high, it’s saturation” is good.
Rafael Mendes: queue depth alone causes too many false positives during deploys (warmup) and incident bot will scream constantly.

Allison Grant: +1. queue depth spikes are common during rolling restarts.
Allison Grant: but backpressure_ratio sustained is rare without real saturation.

Mina Haddad: also: if we highlight saturation, add a line that surfaces current routing policy + fallback enabled/disabled. that’s what oncall needs to decide action.

Jacob Stein: KV cache: what threshold would we use? eviction rate > X/s?

Logan Wright: runtime team suggested: “evictions > 0 for 5m” is already bad for some workloads, but it depends.
Logan Wright: maybe we highlight KV cache only when:
- evictions_rate > 0 AND p95 latency burn > 2x
- OR “kv_cache_hit_rate < 0.8” if available

Aisha Bello: ok, consensus check:
1) Primary highlight: scheduler backpressure_ratio + admission rejects
2) Secondary: queue_depth/queue_wait (supporting)
3) KV cache: highlight only if evictions + latency burn
4) GPU util: include but never highlight as “cause” alone

Miles Thornton: works for bot UX. we can implement “Highlights” block w 1-2 bullets max.
Miles Thornton: everything else in “Signals” block + dashboard links.

Rafael Mendes: pls add note about tuning thresholds per pool type (hosted shared vs dedicated) so we don’t break enterprise trust.

Allison Grant: I’ll paste in proposed wording for the card:
- “Saturation likely (scheduler throttling + rejects). Consider failover / scale / load shed.”
- “Saturation possible (queueing elevated). Check deploy + config changes.”

Aisha Bello: :thumbsup: I’ll update the standard doc + open a ticket for initial thresholds. Logan/Allison can you sanity check numbers once I draft?

Logan Wright: yup. also please keep query bounded: only pull p95 over 5m + last 30m sparkline link, no high-cardinality breakdown.

Caleb Johnson: +1 on bounded queries. per-tenant breakdown from bot will kill us during incidents.

Aisha Bello: cool. action items:
- Aisha: write threshold proposal + bot highlight rules
- Logan/Nadia: confirm metric names available in hosted + dedicated
- Allison: align with alert tuning so bot highlights match paging logic
- Miles: incorporate into bot card layout (highlight vs signals)

Miles Thornton: ack. will wire into v2 card gating (only show highlight if confidence high).

title: #announcements: 1739308055
preview:
Talia Benson: :mega: Internal preview: Dedicated tiers + throughput SLO

We’re ready for an internal preview of the Dedicated tiering + throughput SLO work.

What this is:
• A customer-facing tier taxonomy (Standard / Plus / Premium) with explicit entitlements
• A formal throughput SLO definition + measurement semantics for Dedicated
• Initial shape catalog + tier→shape mapping guidance (by region)
• Draft enablement assets (cheat sheet, PRD/FAQ) + escalation + comms plan for SLO misses

What this is NOT (yet):
• Final contract language / order form text (Legal review in progress)
• Public docs GA (docs draft exists but not ready to share externally)
• “Promise any number you want” — please do not invent custom commitments. Use the tier guidance.

Where to read (internal only):
• PRD (Dedicated throughput SLO + tiers): sources/confluence/product-docs/requirements/dedicated-throughput-slo-prd.json
• Formal SLO definition (metrics/exclusions/alerts): sources/confluence/eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/dedicated-throughput-slo-definition.json
• Tier pricing proposal (v1): sources/confluence/product-docs/pricing-and-packaging/dedicated-pricing-tiers-v1.json
• Seller cheat sheet draft: sources/confluence/sales-enablement/pricing-guidance/dedicated-tier-cheat-sheet.json
• Shape catalog (Dedicated pools by region): sources/confluence/eng-infra/gpu-fleet-and-capacity/hardware-shape-catalog-dedicated.json

High-level decisions (preview):
• Canonical throughput metric: tokens/sec attributed per Dedicated deployment (streaming-safe accounting)
• Measurement window: rolling windows with burn-rate alerting (details in SLO doc)
• Exclusions: customer-side throttling/backpressure, unsupported model/version/ctx settings, and warmup periods (see SLO doc for exact wording)
• Tiers:
  - Standard: throughput floor on a single region pool, best-effort burst, standard support
  - Plus: higher floor + defined burst policy, faster response targets, stronger isolation assumptions
  - Premium: highest floor, warm pool/spare capacity posture, most conservative overload behavior, fastest response targets

Timeline (current plan):
• This week: internal preview + feedback window
• Next 1–2 weeks: finalize tier entitlements/exclusions + pricing guardrails + shape list per region
• Enablement: Sales/CS training sessions start next week (calendar invite from @Ava Chen)
• Limited GA: tiered SLO rollout to a small set of Dedicated customers (beta list being finalized)

Where to send questions / feedback:
• Product/tier definitions: @Lauren Bishop (reply in this thread is fine)
• SLO semantics / measurement / alerts: @Sean Gallagher + @Rafael Mendes
• Shapes / regions / lead times: @Irene Choi + @Vanessa Ortiz
• Pricing/discount guardrails: @Isabelle Laurent (Sales Ops) + @Laura Bennett (Finance, async)
• Enablement materials: @Ava Chen + @Elliot Price

Please keep customer-facing statements to the approved tier language only. If a customer asks for a “throughput guarantee in writing,” route to Lauren + Commercial counsel; we’ll provide the reviewed language.

:thread: Reply here with questions/edge cases you want covered in training.

—

Stephanie Nguyen: Thanks Talia. For AEs: are we ok telling customers “tokens/sec is the commitment” vs “requests/sec”? We’ve got folks quoting QPS today.

Sean Gallagher: Yes — tokens/sec is canonical for Dedicated (more stable across prompt length). We’ll include an AE-friendly translation section ("how to estimate requests/sec") in the cheat sheet, but commitments should be in tokens/sec.

Lauren Bishop: +1. If a customer insists on req/sec, we can map it in scoping as an estimate using their prompt distribution, but the contractual commitment will be tokens/sec.

Isabelle Laurent: Adding: Deal Desk will flag any quote w/ req/sec language. Please keep it tier + tokens/sec.

Ava Chen: Enablement invite going out today. Two sessions (PT + ET friendly). Recording + FAQ will live alongside the cheat sheet.

Dev Patel: For Support/CS: do we have a clear "when do we credit" trigger, or is that still being finalized?

Rafael Mendes: Draft policy exists in the escalation playbook (not final-final). In preview: if we confirm a platform-side SLO breach over the measurement window and the customer was operating within supported config, we’ll follow the standard service credit framework. Final numbers/terms need Legal sign-off.

Lauren Bishop: If you see a customer escalation before terms are final, route through CS leadership + me. We’ll handle on a case-by-case basis (and keep it consistent).

Irene Choi: Infra note: shape availability varies a lot by region right now. Please don’t promise specific GPU types (H100 vs A100) unless it’s in the approved shape catalog for that region + we’ve confirmed supply.

Vanessa Ortiz: +1 and please set expectations on lead times for Premium shapes in us-east + eu-central. We’ll share a “what’s orderable this week” report via ENG-19271 once it’s live.

Allison Grant: Observability: dashboards are in progress. For preview, we can show internal panels for per-deployment throughput floor + saturation signals. Customer-facing console widget will lag a bit.

Elliot Price: Docs heads up: there’s a draft Dedicated tiers + throughput SLO explainer, but please treat as internal-only until we mark it approved. We’re tuning wording (esp. exclusions) to avoid confusion.

Connor O'Brien: Rollout plan: tier-aware routing/admission control is behind a feature flag. We’ll canary on a couple internal deployments first, then a small beta set. Please don’t self-serve toggle anything; ping release.

Redwood Deploy Bot: FYI: `routing-tier-to-pool-selector` is currently enabled in `staging` only. Feature flag `dedicated_tier_pool_selector` remains OFF in prod.

Stephanie Nguyen: Can we get 2–3 canned talk tracks for “burst” vs “committed” so reps stop overpromising?

Ava Chen: Yup — we’re adding a one-pager + slides with exact wording + example scenarios (steady-state vs burst event). Will share before the first training.

Dev Patel: One more: customers sometimes use multiple models in one Dedicated deployment. Is that supported in the SLO?

Sean Gallagher: Supported, but the commitment is per deployment and assumes they stay within the supported model set/config for that shape. If they mix models with very different perf profiles, they may not hit their own expectations. We’ll spell out “pinned model/version” guidance + when we recommend separate deployments.

Lauren Bishop: We’re likely to recommend “one deployment per primary model” for Plus/Premium to keep the guarantee predictable. If you have a real account w/ multi-model needs, send it my way — we can advise on topology.

Talia Benson: Keep the edge cases coming — we’ll bake into the FAQ + training. Thanks all.
